{"query":"$1.72 per ticket overcharge treble damages total amount Live Nation Ticketmaster Clayton Act jury verdict 2026","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1633,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/after-the-verdict-navigating-the-live-nationticketmaster-antitrust-fallout","title":"After the Verdict: Navigating the Live Nation/Ticketmaster Antitrust Fallout | Crowell & Moring LLP","content":"The plaintiffs alleged that Live Nation controls 78% of the large amphitheaters used by artists and, through Ticketmaster, 86% of primary ticketing (i.e., initial sale of tickets) at major concert venues.
On April 15, 2026, the jury returned a verdict for the plaintiffs after four days of deliberations.
Under the Clayton Act, treble damages are mandatory upon a finding of antitrust liability, meaning the trebled figure could approach $450 million before offsets.
The Court deferred ruling on that motion while noting significant concerns with the damages expert’s analysis.
United States’ Position: The United States intends to file the Proposed Final Judgment, Stipulation and Order, and Explanation of Procedures under the Antitrust Procedures and Penalties Act by late May 2026."},{"url":"https://www.thompsoncoburn.com/insights/live-nation-and-ticketmaster-found-liable-for-antitrust-violations-by-federal-jury/","title":"Live Nation and Ticketmaster Found Liable for Antitrust Violations by Federal Jury | Thompson Coburn LLP","content":"Apr 28, 2026 — $1.72 multiplied by the 400 million tickets they claim were sold at inflated prices could yield $688 million in class damages (over $2 billion ...
The April 15, 2026 verdict followed a five-week trial in a case brought by 33 states and the District of Columbia (collectively “States”).
The DOJ settlement, if approved, also provides significant relief—Live Nation retains Ticketmaster but must open its venues to rival ticketing services such as SeatGeek; it must divest certain amphitheaters; and it must cap ticket fees at 15%.
Live Nation has estimated that the States’ treble damages could reach $450 million.
Meanwhile, attorneys for a certified class action in the Central District of California (Judge Wu) undoubtedly have their own calculations: $1.72 multiplied by the 400 million tickets they claim were sold at inflated prices could yield $688 million in class damages (over $2 billion after automatic trebling)."},{"url":"https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/live-nation-verdict-finds-1-143923408.html","title":"Live Nation Verdict Finds $1.72 Per Ticket Overcharge In Antitrust Case","content":"In that context, a New York federal jury on April 15 concluded that Live Nation illegally monopolized the live events industry and overcharged concertgoers, estimating an average overcharge of $1.72 per ticket tied to its position through Ticketmaster."},{"url":"https://time.com/article/2026/04/16/live-nation-federal-antitrust-verdict-explainer/","title":"How Live Nation Verdict May Affect Ticket Prices","content":"The verdict came about two years after the Biden Administration’s Justice Department and nearly 40 state attorneys general sued Live Nation over its unrivaled control on ticket sales and venues.
The jury also found that Ticketmaster overcharged $1.72 per ticket in 21 states and in D.C. because of Live Nation’s anticompetitive practices.
Omeed Assefi, acting assistant attorney general for the Justice Department’s antitrust division, called the jury verdict “a fantastic outcome for the American people” in a statement sent to the media: “DOJ and some states settled their case and got instant relief."},{"url":"https://www.witn.com/2026/04/15/jury-finds-that-ticketmaster-live-nation-had-an-anticompetitive-monopoly-over-big-concert-venues/","title":"Jury finds that Ticketmaster and Live Nation had an anticompetitive monopoly over big concert venues","content":"Among other things, the jury found Ticketmaster’s anticompetitive practices led to people in 22 states paying an extra $1.72 per ticket, which the judge could order the companies to pay back.
Live Nation CEO Michael Rapino testified, answering questions about matters including the company’s Taylor Swift ticket debacle in 2022.
In its statement, Live Nation said the jury’s award of $1.72 per ticket applied to “a limited number of tickets” sold at 257 venues and representing about 20% of total tickets sold.
New York Attorney General Letitia James called the verdict “a landmark victory.”"}]}

{"query":"$1.72 per ticket overcharge treble damages total amount Live Nation Ticketmaster Clayton Act jury verdict 2026","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.tradingview.com/news/gurufocus:55e937d21094b:0-live-nation-verdict-finds-1-72-per-ticket-overcharge-in-antitrust-case","title":"Live Nation Verdict Finds $1.72 Per Ticket Overcharge In Antitrust Case — TradingView News","content":"Title: Live Nation Verdict Finds $1.72 Per Ticket Overcharge In Antitrust Case — TradingView News
# Live Nation Verdict Finds $1.72 Per Ticket Overcharge In Antitrust Case. A U.S. jury verdict against Live Nation Entertainment LYV is emerging at a time when the economics of live music have already shifted meaningfully, with touring now accounting for roughly 70% of artists' income as physical album sales have declined and streaming revenue has proven less robust. This structural shift, combined with a post-pandemic rebound in demand for live events, has contributed to a steady increase in ticket prices over the past two decades. Data cited in the case shows average face values for top-grossing artists rising from $25.81 in 1996 to $136.45 in 2024, while fees have also expanded, averaging about 27% of ticket prices in the primary market. At the same time, the growth of resale platforms has introduced additional pricing pressure, with secondary market fees ranging from 20% to 56%, potentially reinforcing higher price expectations across the ecosystem. In that context, a New York federal jury on April 15 concluded that Live Nation illegally monopolized the live events industry and overcharged concertgoers, estimating an average overcharge of $1.72 per ticket tied to its position through Ticketmaster. A separate coalition of 33 states and Washington, DC continued litigation, arguing that Live Nation's scaleestimated by plaintiffs to exceed 80% of the markethas allowed it to secure exclusive venue agreements and limit competition from alternative platforms such as StubHub and SeatGeek, potentially contributing to higher fees and overall ticket costs. Live Nation has disputed those claims, maintaining that artists determine ticket face values while venues set fee structures, and noting that around 80% of the concerts it promotes take place at venues it does not own or operate. The company is expected to appeal the verdict, but the outcome may introduce uncertainty around its integrated model spanning promotion, venues, and ticketing, and could lead to structural remedies that reshape pricing dynamics and competitive conditions in the live entertainment industry. ## Latest news. ## More news from GuruFocus. # Live Nation Verdict Finds $1.72 Per Ticket Overcharge In Antitrust Case. A U.S. jury verdict against Live Nation Entertainment (NYSE:LYV) is emerging at a time when the economics of live music have already shifted meaningfully, with touring now accounting for roughly 70% of artists' income as physical album sales have declined and streaming revenue has proven less robust. This structural shift, combined with a post-pandemic rebound in demand for live events, has contributed to a steady increase in ticket prices over the past two decades. Data cited in the case shows average face values for top-grossing artists rising from $25.81 in 1996 to $136.45 in 2024, while fees have also expanded, averaging about 27% of ticket prices in the primary market. At the same time, the growth of resale platforms has introduced additional pricing pressure, with secondary market fees ranging from 20% to 56%, potentially reinforcing higher price expectations across the ecosystem. In that context, a New York federal jury on April 15 concluded that Live Nation illegally monopolized the live events industry and overcharged concertgoers, estimating an average overcharge of $1.72 per ticket tied to its position through Ticketmaster. The case traces back to a 2024 lawsuit brought by the Justice Department and more than three dozen states, although a partial settlement was reached in March 2026 with federal authorities and several states. A separate coalition of 33 states and Washington, DC continued litigation, arguing that Live Nation's scaleestimated by plaintiffs to exceed 80% of the markethas allowed it to secure exclusive venue agreements and limit competition from alternative platforms such as StubHub and SeatGeek, potentially contributing to higher fees and overall ticket costs. Live Nation has disputed those claims, maintaining that artists determine ticket face values while venues set fee structures, and noting that around 80% of the concerts it promotes take place at venues it does not own or operate. The company is expected to appeal the verdict, but the outcome may introduce uncertainty around its integrated model spanning promotion, venues, and ticketing, and could lead to structural remedies that reshape pricing dynamics and competitive conditions in the live entertainment industry.","score":0.8405867,"raw_content":null,"domain":"yahoo.com","popularity_score":9.68192905388578,"main_topic":"government"},{"url":"https://winston.com/en/insights-news/winston-secures-transformative-antitrust-verdict-for-states-in-live-nation-and-ticketmaster-trial","title":"Winston Secures Transformative Antitrust Verdict for States in Live Nation and Ticketmaster Trial | Winston & Strawn","content":"Title: Winston Secures Transformative Antitrust Verdict for States in Live Nation and Ticketmaster Trial | Winston & Strawn
# Winston Secures Transformative Antitrust Verdict for States in Live Nation and Ticketmaster Trial. Winston Secures Transformative Antitrust Verdict for States in Live Nation and Ticketmaster Trial. #### Related Capabilities. A Winston & Strawn team, led by Jeffrey Kessler, secured an antitrust verdict on behalf of more than 30 states and the District of Columbia, with a Manhattan federal jury finding Live Nation Entertainment and Ticketmaster liable for violating federal and state antitrust laws in a decision that has the potential to transform the concert, music, and ticketing industry. The jury concluded that Ticketmaster unlawfully maintained a monopoly in the primary ticketing market for major concert venues and that Live Nation unlawfully monopolized the large amphitheater market, compelling artists to use its promotion services to access its venues. Jurors further found that this conduct resulted in higher ticket fees, leading to an approximate $1.72 overcharge per ticket for consumers. “It’s a great day for antitrust law. It’s a great day for consumers,” Jeffrey said following the verdict. “This case is a tribute to the 34 states and the District of Columbia who carried this case forward, and it was my great honor to be working with them together on this.”. The verdict follows a five-week trial pursued by a coalition of 33 states and Washington, D.C. that rejected the Department of Justice’s proposed settlement with Live Nation earlier in the proceedings. The case now moves to a remedies phase, where the court will determine injunctive relief, treble damages, and civil penalties. # Live Nation Monopoly Verdict 2026, Jury Rules Ticketmaster Overcharged Fans What It Means for You. If the private class action also resolves favorably, a separate payout covering tickets since 2010 — more than 400 million tickets — could push the total into the billions. **DOJ Settlement (separate):** During the trial, the Justice Department settled separately, allowing Live Nation to keep Ticketmaster in exchange for behavioral changes — including divesting 13 amphitheater agreements and capping ticketing service fees at 15%. **Live Nation’s Response:** The company stated the verdict “is not the last word” and confirmed it will file motions challenging both the liability and damages findings, with appeals to follow if necessary.
